label: verify-18-cal-app-4th-781
system:
You are the citation verifier in a legal-exploration pipeline. You are given one authority as cited by the pipeline's components, plus snippets showing how it was used. Using web search (prefer primary/official sources: leginfo.legislature.ca.gov, courts.ca.gov, law.justia.com, casetext.com, codes.findlaw.com, fppc.ca.gov), determine:
1. Does the authority exist?
2. Is the citation form correct (reporter cite, year, section number)?
3. Does it plausibly stand for what the snippets use it for?

Statuses: VERIFIED (exists, cite essentially right, usage consistent with what you found); MISCHARACTERIZED (exists but does not support the claimed use, or the cite attaches to the wrong case/section); NOT_FOUND (cannot locate after genuine searching); CONTESTED (real sources point in conflicting directions, or scope is genuinely unsettled); UNCLEAR (could not determine within a reasonable search).

You are NOT determining whether the authority is still good law (no Shepardizing) — say what you actually confirmed, no more. Be strict: a close-but-wrong reporter cite or a holding stretched past what sources show is MISCHARACTERIZED, not VERIFIED.
prompt:
## Authority as cited (variants seen in the documents)

- 18 Cal.App.4th 781

## How the documents used it

[delphi] …)
- Gov. Code § 54952.2(c) — technological devices/electronic communications (2, one with a differing description)
- *Roberts v. City of Palmdale* (1993) 5 Cal.4th 363 (2)
- *Frazer v. Dixon Unified School Dist.* (1993) 18 Cal.App.4th 781 (2)

**Cited by only one panelist:**
- Cal. Const. art. I, § 3(b) (Prop 59)
- Gov. Code §§ 54960 and 54960.5 — standing and fee-shifting
- *Sacramento Newspaper Guild v. Sacramento County Bd. of Supervisors* (1968) 263 …

[dialectic] …fing, said "Member X was persuaded by the density-bonus argument," any Brown Act practitioner in California would say the safe harbor was lost and a serial meeting occurred. *Frazer v. Dixon Unified School Dist.* (1993) 18 Cal.App.4th 781 supports the broader proposition that a chain of individual, one-on-one conversations among board members about a specific pending personnel/policy matter — with no full-body gathering — can itself constitute a Brown Ac…

[naive_sonnet] …s:

- **Majority**: 4 of 5 members — satisfied on its face. The doctrine (built around "hub and spoke" and "daisy chain" fact patterns, e.g., the line of authority following *Frazer v. Dixon Unified School Dist.* (1993) 18 Cal.App.4th 781 — verify pin cite) was developed *precisely* to reach situations where no two members were ever in a room together, but a common conduit passed positions between them serially, over time. The absence of any simultaneous…

## Context

The underlying question is a California public-agency hypothetical: q1 — The AI assistant as serial-meeting intermediary (Brown Act).

Verify this authority per your instructions.

canonical_cite.description: the correct full citation, if determinable

note.description: 1-3 sentences: what was confirmed or refuted, and the correct cite

text: {"status":"MISCHARACTERIZED","canonical_cite":"Frazer v. Dixon Unified School Dist. (1993) 18 Cal.App.4th 781 (1st Dist.)","note":"The case exists and the cite (18 Cal.App.4th 781, 1993) is correct. But its holding is that an actual joint gathering of a board quorum (a Curriculum Council/Board work session where members together viewed a videotape) was a \"meeting\" because it involved \"collective acquisition and exchange of facts\" — it did not require verbal deliberation. The 'dialectic' and 'naive_sonnet' snippets instead cite Frazer for a \"hub-and-spoke\"/\"daisy chain\" serial-meeting proposition (no full-body gathering, information passed via a common conduit) — that is a different doctrine associated with cases like Stockton Newspapers, Inc. v. Redevelopment Agency and later Gov. Code § 54952.2(b), and available sources indicate Frazer itself distinguished its facts from that serial-meeting scenario rather than establishing it. So the citation is real but stretched to support a proposition the case doesn't stand for.","source_urls":["https://law.justia.com/cases/california/court-of-appeal/4th/18/781.html","https://firstamendmentcoalition.org/asked-and-answered/collective-concurrences-serial-meeting-and-the-brown-act/","https://oag.ca.gov/system/files/media/the-brown-act.pdf"]}

data.canonical_cite: Frazer v. Dixon Unified School Dist. (1993) 18 Cal.App.4th 781 (1st Dist.)
data.note: The case exists and the cite (18 Cal.App.4th 781, 1993) is correct. But its holding is that an actual joint gathering of a board quorum (a Curriculum Council/Board work session where members together viewed a videotape) was a "meeting" because it involved "collective acquisition and exchange of facts" — it did not require verbal deliberation. The 'dialectic' and 'naive_sonnet' snippets instead cite Frazer for a "hub-and-spoke"/"daisy chain" serial-meeting proposition (no full-body gathering, information passed via a common conduit) — that is a different doctrine associated with cases like Stockton Newspapers, Inc. v. Redevelopment Agency and later Gov. Code § 54952.2(b), and available sources indicate Frazer itself distinguished its facts from that serial-meeting scenario rather than establishing it. So the citation is real but stretched to support a proposition the case doesn't stand for.
